Tupac Amaru Shakur was born on June 16, 1971, in East Harlem, New York City.
His mother, Afeni Shakur, was a member of the Black Panther Party and was acquitted of conspiracy charges just one month before Tupac was born.
Tupac was named after Túpac Amaru II, an 18th-century Peruvian revolutionary who led an indigenous uprising against Spanish colonial rule.
He attended the Baltimore School for the Arts where he studied acting, poetry, jazz, and ballet.
At Baltimore School for the Arts, Tupac befriended Jada Pinkett (now Jada Pinkett Smith), and they remained close friends for life.
Tupac's first major rap performance was a freestyle battle at a school talent show in Baltimore — he won.
He moved to Marin City, California as a teenager and lived in poverty, sometimes going without food.
Tupac joined Digital Underground in 1990 as a roadie and backup dancer before getting his first rap feature.
His debut verse on record was on Digital Underground's 'Same Song' in 1991.
His debut solo album '2Pacalypse Now' was released in November 1991 and immediately drew controversy for its raw political content.
Vice President Dan Quayle publicly called for '2Pacalypse Now' to be pulled from shelves after a young man cited it during a murder trial.
Tupac appeared in the 1992 film 'Juice' as the menacing Bishop — critics praised it as a breakthrough performance.
He starred alongside Janet Jackson in 'Poetic Justice' (1993), his first major Hollywood leading role.
Tupac had a minor role in 'Nothing but Trouble' (1991) alongside Digital Underground before his film career took off.
The album 'Strictly 4 My N.I.G.G.A.Z.' (1993) went platinum and featured 'I Get Around' and 'Keep Ya Head Up.'
'Keep Ya Head Up' sampled the Staple Singers and was dedicated to women — a recurring theme in Tupac's music.
Tupac was deeply influenced by his mother's activism and incorporated Black Power themes throughout his catalog.
He was shot five times in the lobby of a New York recording studio on November 30, 1994 — and survived.
After the 1994 shooting, Tupac accused Sean 'Diddy' Combs and The Notorious B.I.G. of prior knowledge.
Tupac signed to Death Row Records in 1995 while serving time in Clinton Correctional Facility.
Suge Knight of Death Row Records posted Tupac's $1.4 million bail in exchange for a three-album deal.
While incarcerated, Tupac read extensively, including works by Machiavelli — later adopting the alias 'Makaveli.'
The name Makaveli was an anagram of 'AM ALIVE K' — fueling conspiracy theories after his death.
'Me Against the World' (1995) debuted at #1 on the Billboard 200 while Tupac was in prison — making him the first artist to do so.
'Me Against the World' went double platinum and is widely regarded as one of the greatest rap albums ever made.
Tupac recorded the double album 'All Eyez on Me' in just two weeks after his release from prison in 1996.
'All Eyez on Me' is the first double-disc album in hip-hop history to be released simultaneously.
'All Eyez on Me' debuted at #1, went diamond (10x platinum), and remains one of the best-selling rap albums ever.
'California Love' featuring Dr. Dre became Tupac's first #1 single on the Billboard Hot 100.
The 'California Love' music video was directed by Hype Williams and featured a Mad Max-inspired dystopian setting.
'How Do U Want It' also hit #1 on the Hot 100 in 1996, giving Tupac back-to-back number ones.
Tupac wrote poetry as a teenager; a collection titled 'The Rose That Grew from Concrete' was published posthumously in 1999.
He wrote 'Dear Mama,' one of the most celebrated tribute songs ever recorded, about his complex relationship with his mother Afeni.
'Dear Mama' was added to the Library of Congress National Recording Registry in 2023 — the first rap song to receive that honor.
Tupac recorded over 200 unreleased songs during his lifetime; many were released posthumously.
He used multiple aliases including Makaveli, 2Pac, and Big Syke (in collaborative contexts).
Tupac was shot and killed on September 13, 1996, in Las Vegas, six days after being shot four times in a drive-by following the Mike Tyson vs. Bruce Seldon fight.
He was 25 years old when he died — one of the most famous ages in rock and rap history.
The Las Vegas shooting occurred on Flamingo Road near Koval Lane on the night of September 7, 1996.
Suge Knight was driving the BMW when Tupac was shot; Knight suffered only a minor head wound.
No one was ever charged with Tupac's murder. The case remains officially unsolved.
Tupac sold over 75 million records worldwide, making him one of the best-selling music artists of all time.
He has more Billboard 200 #1 albums than most artists who were alive for their entire careers.
Tupac was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame in 2017 in his first year of eligibility.
He was the first solo rapper to be inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.
Tupac appeared on the cover of Rolling Stone four times — twice while alive and twice posthumously.
His feud with The Notorious B.I.G. and Bad Boy Records defined the East Coast–West Coast hip-hop rivalry of the mid-1990s.
Despite the feud, Tupac and Biggie had been close friends earlier in their careers.
Tupac wrote 'Hit 'Em Up,' widely considered the most vicious diss track in hip-hop history, targeting Biggie and Bad Boy.
Tupac claimed to have slept with Biggie's then-wife Faith Evans on the track 'Hit 'Em Up.'
He was a gifted actor — director John Singleton said Tupac was the most naturally talented actor he'd ever worked with.
Tupac was cast in 'Bullet' (1996) alongside Mickey Rourke, a role he shot while on work release from prison.
He was set to star in 'Gridlock'd' and 'Gang Related,' both released posthumously in 1997.
'Gang Related' was Tupac's final film; he filmed it in the months before his death.
Tupac nearly starred in Oliver Stone's 'Natural Born Killers' before the role went elsewhere.
He auditioned for the role of Bubba in 'Forrest Gump' but didn't get it.
Tupac had a 3.0 GPA while studying at Baltimore School for the Arts, despite his turbulent home life.
He wrote his first rap at age 12, a piece called 'The Lunatic' dedicated to his mother.
Tupac's father, Billy Garland, was largely absent; he reconnected with Tupac only briefly before his death.
His stepfather, Mutulu Shakur, was a convicted bank robber and Black Liberation Army member — a figure who deeply influenced him.
Tupac's godfather is Geronimo Pratt, a Black Panther imprisoned for 27 years for a murder conviction later overturned.
Tupac was passionate about chess and reportedly played it regularly in prison.
He kept a journal throughout his life and wrote poetry almost daily.
Tupac collaborated with artists across genres — from Dr. Dre and Snoop Dogg to Nate Dogg and Danny Boy.
He was close friends with the Outlawz, a rap collective he formed in 1995; they were originally called the Dramacydal.
Members of the Outlawz named themselves after world leaders Tupac admired — Yaki Kadafi, Kastro, Kadafi, and Napoleon.
After his death, the Outlawz revealed they smoked Tupac's ashes mixed into a blunt, as per his wishes.
Tupac was a licensed firearms owner in California, citing death threats as justification.
He was convicted of sexual assault in 1995 and sentenced to 18 months in prison — a charge he consistently denied.
While in prison, Tupac converted to Islam and was given the name Malik.
Tupac wore a cross necklace and identified with themes of Christianity despite his complex spiritual views.
He was a vocal critic of police brutality long before it became a mainstream media conversation.
Tupac's Oakland anthem 'Holler If Ya Hear Me' was a rallying cry for Black communities facing systemic oppression.
He once said: 'I'm not saying I'm gonna change the world, but I guarantee that I will spark the brain that will.'
Tupac was deeply influenced by Malcolm X, whose speeches he studied and quoted in interviews.
He launched a charitable initiative in 1995 called Tupac Amaru Shakur Foundation, later expanded by Afeni.
The Tupac Amaru Shakur Center for the Arts opened in Stone Mountain, Georgia in 2005.
Tupac was known for memorizing the lyrics of other rappers and reciting them verbatim in interviews.
He appeared in the music video for Digital Underground's 'Same Song' dressed in drag as part of a skit.
Tupac's half-sister Sekyiwa Shakur has spoken extensively about his life in interviews and documentaries.
He wore bulletproof vests in public regularly after the 1994 shooting — he was not wearing one the night he was killed.
Tupac signed to Interscope Records through Jive Records before eventually landing at Death Row.
He improvised much of his verse on Dr. Dre's 'California Love' in the studio — Dre was reportedly stunned.
Tupac's posthumous album 'R U Still Down? (Remember Me)' (1997) went double platinum.
The posthumous album 'Until the End of Time' (2001) debuted at #1 and went triple platinum.
Tupac recorded the Makaveli album 'The Don Killuminati: The 7 Day Theory' in just three days.
The 7 Day Theory was released just two months after his death under the alias Makaveli — fueling immortality theories.
Tupac loved cartoons and reportedly watched them regularly as a form of relaxation.
He was a fast talker in real life — friends say conversations with him were like trying to keep up with a freight train.
Tupac got his first tattoo at 16 — '2PAC' across his stomach in block letters.
He later added 'Thug Life' across his abdomen, one of the most iconic tattoos in pop culture history.
'Thug Life' stood for 'The Hate U Give Little Infants F***s Everybody' — a social commentary, not a glorification of crime.
Angie Thomas named her 2017 bestselling novel 'The Hate U Give' after the meaning behind 'Thug Life.'
Tupac's likeness has been used in several hologram and digital resurrection performances, most famously at Coachella 2012.
The Coachella 2012 hologram performed 'Hail Mary' and 'California Love' alongside Snoop Dogg — it became a viral sensation.
Tupac has influenced countless artists including Kendrick Lamar, J. Cole, Drake, Eminem, and Beyoncé.
Kendrick Lamar said Tupac's 'Me Against the World' is the reason he became a rapper.
Barack Obama cited Tupac as one of his favorite artists while on the campaign trail in 2008.
A street in Atlanta was renamed 'Tupac Shakur Drive' in his honor.
Tupac's mother Afeni Shakur preserved his legacy until her death in 2016, managing his estate and foundation.
